summary:
Peritoneal describes tissue lining the abdomen containing minimal fluid; thus, unlike other body fluids such as blood plasma or interstitial liquid, it doesn't represent a discrete storage area but instead demarcates anatomical spaces hosting variable quantities based on health status. This distinguishes it among typical classifications of bodily liquids' containers. To directly answer your query concisely:

The peritoneal cavity houses only about 50-100mL under normal conditions, making it more accurately described by function within anatomy versus being considered one specific 'compartment'. Thus, it isn’t classified similarly alongside major categories defining larger pools of circulating/interacting biological waters inside our bodies. 

This summary captures essential details while maintaining brevity focused strictly around core medical concepts pertinent here without extraneous information. Let me know if you need any adjustments!
